01 · The Problem

Creators had good ideas.
Getting them made was the hard part.

VeeFly helped 56,000+ creators grow on YouTube. But nobody had fixed the 4–6 hours between having an idea and being ready to film.

Creators juggled 3–5 tools and AI made it worse — generic content for any channel, no awareness of who they were writing for.

"I have ChatGPT, Descript, TubeBuddy, and Google Docs open at once. By the time I stitch it all together, I've lost the energy I had when the idea hit."
— Arjun, Solo Creator, 180K subs
"Every AI tool ignores 2 years of data about what my audience loves. I have to re-brief it like a new intern every single time."
— Sarah, Content Lead, SaaS Brand
02 · Research

38 conversations.
One clear pattern.

I talked to creators one-on-one, watched them work in real time, and ran a survey with 64 people. The problem wasn't that AI tools wrote badly. They just had no idea who they were writing for.

38 Interviews 64-person survey Screen-share sessions 14 channel audits
"The AI gives me content that sounds like someone else's channel. I rewrite everything — which defeats the whole point."
— Arjun, 180K subs
"We have 2 years of channel data on what works. Every AI tool ignores it completely. I re-brief it like a new intern every time."
— Sarah, Content Lead
83%

Said AI outputs needed full rewrites — generic content, wrong audience.

74%

Wanted AI that understood YouTube structure, not just text generation.

50%

Of production time was scripting. The single biggest bottleneck.

0

Tools that used a creator's own channel data to personalise output.

What we were solving for

Six friction points.
One broken system.

01

No Channel Memory

AI starts from zero every session. Years of data, never used.

4–6h

Scripting Bottleneck

Most of it wrestling tools, not creating.

3–5

Tool Fragmentation

Every tool switch loses context and momentum.

69%

Generic Output

Didn't match niche, tone, or what their audience actually cares about.

0

Analytics Blindness

Performance data sits unused with no way to turn it into decisions.

3×

Re-work Cost

Editing AI output took 3× longer than generating it.

03 · How the AI Learns Your Channel

The AI reads your channel
before you type a word.

Connect once. The AI scans your videos, spots what performs best, and uses that to shape every suggestion and script it generates.

Showing creators their own data explained why suggestions made sense — and built trust faster than any onboarding copy.

"The best brief is the one the AI writes from your own data — before you open the prompt box."
— Design principle, week 3
📡
Channel Scan

Pulls your full upload history, titles, tags, watch time and retention data via YouTube API.

→
🧠
Pattern Analysis

Groups videos into topic clusters. Spots which formats, hooks and structures work best for your audience.

→
✨
Personalised Output

Suggestions ranked by predicted performance. Scripts shaped by your proven topics and audience tone.

04 · What We Changed and Why

Every decision was
tested, not assumed.

We ran 3 rounds of usability testing. Each round changed something. Here are the five biggest shifts.

"The first version felt like a very fast form. We want it to feel like working with someone who already knows your channel."
— Usability test participant, Round 1
First approach

Generic trending topics as suggestions

→
What we built

Suggestions from their own channel data

Ideas ranked by what their own audience responds to.

Why?  Personalised chips had 3× higher tap rate vs generic in prototype tests.
First approach

A form — Topic / Audience / Tone fields

→
What we built

One open text box + smart suggestion chips

The AI already has context. Describe the idea and go.

Why?  7 of 9 people abandoned at the "Tone" dropdown in Round 1. Session starts up 40% without it.
First approach

Full script delivered all at once

→
What we built

Streamed section by section

Hook → Body → CTA, each part editable as it appears.

Why?  6 of 8 people hit regenerate without reading the full script. Section streaming fixed that.
First approach

Separate tabs — Script / Voice / SEO

→
What we built

One scrollable document

Everything in one thread — one content package, not three separate outputs.

Why?  Tab-switching reintroduced the exact fragmentation we were solving. 5 of 7 preferred the scroll.
First approach

Voice names in a dropdown

→
What we built

Visual grid with tone labels and audio preview

Personality tag + a play button to hear it before choosing.

Why?  8 of 10 regenerated after hearing the wrong voice. Previews cut that by 40%.
05 · The Screens

Every screen with the
thinking behind it.

1
Channel state visible

"56 videos analysed" — trust before they even type.

2
One open input

No form fields. The AI already has the context.

3
Ranked suggestions

Ranked by this creator's own performance data. 🔥 = high-confidence picks.

4
Session history

Pick up where you left off or plan a content calendar.

5
Credits upfront

Reduces surprise charges and helps creators plan sessions.

6
Clear nav split

Two modes clearly separated — no confusion about what you're doing.

1
One scrollable document

One document, not three separate AI outputs.

2
Edit in place

Edit inline. Faster than regenerating from scratch.

3
Structure from your data

Structure learned from the creator's own top-performing videos.

4
Voiceover always visible

Always visible so you can listen without losing your place.

5
Prompt stays visible

Original prompt stays visible — easy to check if the output matched the brief.

6
Refine in the same thread

Ask edits conversationally: "make the hook punchier," "shorten Part 2."

1
Tone labels not tech specs

Personality labels, not tech specs. Creators think in vibes.

2
Hear it before you pick it

Hear it before you pick it. Cut wrong-voice regenerations by 40%.

3
Avatars make voices memorable

Avatars make voices memorable — "Rosey" not "Voice ID 2847."

4
Grid not a dropdown

Grid beats dropdown — creators chose 2× faster in eye-tracking tests.

5
Colour-coded personality

Colour-coded by personality — filter by vibe at a glance.

6
Modal keeps context

Script stays visible behind the overlay. Close and you're right back.

"First AI tool that understood I was making content for my specific audience — not just generating text for anyone."

Team

"The suggestions alone cut our planning time in half. We went from 3-day turnarounds to publishing the same week."

Platform signal

"AI users with personalised suggestions showed 2.4× higher weekly engagement — our strongest retention lever."

07 · What I Learned

Four things this project
changed for me.

Not theory. Things the evidence here forced me to actually believe.

"Designing with AI is different from designing for AI. The AI is a collaborator — and the interface has to make that feel true."
— Post-launch note
01

The best brief is written from data, not by the user

Asking creators to describe their niche every time was friction in disguise. When the AI already knew it from channel data, the whole experience shifted.

02

Latency is something you design around, not through

Streaming section by section turned the wait into "watching it think." Same output, completely different experience of getting there.

03

Trust in AI is built in small moments, not overall quality

Creators didn't evaluate AI holistically — they had checkpoints. A suggestion that matched their topic, a hook that sounded like them. Those micro-moments built more trust than any quality improvement.

04

Scope protection needs evidence, not just conviction

When the team pushed for thumbnails, the research said scripting was 50% of friction and thumbnails weren't in the top five. Research doesn't just inform design decisions — it defends them.
